title: Coherence limitations of a Fourier-engineered $\cos(2\varphi)$ transmon qubit
authors: Nataliia K. Zhurbina, Siddharth Singh, Lukas J. Splitthoff, Eugene Y. Huang, Figen Yilmaz, A. Mert Bozkurt, Christian Kraglund Andersen
abstract: Intrinsically protected superconducting qubits are a promising route toward enhancing coherence times and advancing hardware towards applications in quantum computing. The $\cos(2\varphi)$ qubit achieves protection against qubit relaxation by allowing only the coherent tunneling of pairs of Cooper pairs, resulting in Cooper-pair parity symmetry and thereby suppressing charge-induced errors. In this work, we experimentally realize a $\cos(2\varphi)$ qubit by Fourier engineering the energy-phase relation in a multi-junction superconducting circuit. Using an interference-based architecture, we are able to suppress the odd harmonics of an effective qubit potential and we observe good agreement between the measured transition spectrum and the effective theoretical model. We further investigate the energy relaxation time as a function of external flux and find that the qubit lifetime at the flux symmetry point is limited by $1/f$ flux noise. This strong sensitivity arises from residual fluctuations in the first harmonic, which possesses a large prefactor despite being nominally canceled. In contrast, a fluxonium qubit with a similar energy spectrum and noise amplitude is less affected by flux noise, highlighting a key challenge for interference-based protection schemes.
summary: This paper experimentally realizes a cos(2phi) transmon qubit using Fourier engineering to suppress charge errors. It demonstrates that while the design successfully suppresses the first harmonic, the qubit remains highly sensitive to 1/f flux noise, which limits its coherence compared to fluxonium qubits.
main_problem: The study investigates the coherence limitations of a Fourier-engineered cos(2phi) transmon qubit, specifically identifying why 1/f flux noise limits the qubit lifetime at the flux symmetry point.
main_result: The researchers found that while the device achieves a protected regime, the qubit lifetime is limited by 1/f flux noise due to residual first-harmonic fluctuations, making it more sensitive to flux noise than a fluxonium qubit.
method: The authors used two-tone spectroscopy, resonator spectroscopy, and time-domain measurements (Rabi oscillations, T1) combined with a numerical N-level decoherence model and Fermi's Golden Rule to analyze noise contributions.
model_or_system: A multi-junction superconducting circuit using an interference-based architecture (a 'big' SQUID loop and a 'small' SQUID loop) designed to engineer a cos(2phi) potential via Fourier engineering.
key_observables: Qubit transition frequency (f01), energy relaxation time (T1), resonator frequency shifts, and charge matrix elements.
important_parameters: EC/h = 0.21 GHz, EJ/EC >> 50, 1/f flux noise amplitude A1/f = 1.5e-5 Phi0, and effective Josephson energy EJ approx 40 GHz.
main_assumptions: The model assumes the two flux noise channels are uncorrelated, uses the Born-Oppenheimer approximation for internal mode energy, and assumes the system is in thermal equilibrium at 10 mK.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the circuit architecture and device SEM; Fig 2 shows resonator frequency shifts; Fig 3 displays the transition spectrum and visualized wavefunctions; Fig 4 compares measured T1 to modeled noise contributions; Fig S4 compares 2-level vs N-level models.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the protected qubit concept, describes the experimental fabrication and device architecture, presents spectroscopic characterization of the engineered potential, analyzes T1 relaxation as a function of flux, compares noise sources via modeling, and concludes with a comparison to fluxonium.
why_interesting: This paper is relevant to open quantum systems researchers as it provides a detailed experimental study of how specific noise spectral densities (1/f flux noise) couple to a non-standard Hamiltonian and limit the coherence of a protected quantum state.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Qubit circuit and device design. (a) Idealized circuit implementing a cos(2φ)-protected qubit. The cross element enables the tunneling of pairs of Cooper pairs, generating an effective cos(2φ) potential. The tunneling element is addition- ally shunted by a large capacitor. (b) Top: Interference-based implementation of the cos(2φ) element using SNS junctions. Bottom: Schematic of the fabricated device, where the left SQUID arm forms an effectively fixed-energy SNS junction using two Josephson junctions in series. The right arm also implements an effective SNS junction with a single Josephson junction in series with a SQUID loop, such that the effective SNS junction parameters are...

FIG. 2. (a) Measured resonator frequency shift as a function of the external fluxes, after cross-talk calibration. Horizontal traces (with markers) correspond to additionally measured line cuts, ordered from top to bottom with increasing cos(2φ) contribution. (b) Simulated resonator frequency shift using Eq. (8). (c–f) Line cuts of (a) for Φctrl equal to 0.406 Φ0, 0.395 Φ0, 0.378 Φ0, and 0.367 Φ0, respectively, overlaid with simulated resonator shifts. Solid lines indicate transitions from the ground state, while dotted lines correspond to tran- sitions from the first excited state.

FIG. 3. (a–d) Two-tone qubit spectroscopy corresponding to selected measurement points as a function of Φbias, see the annotations of Fig. 2(a). Additionally, in solid lines, we show the fitted spectra. To avoid obscuring the data, the fit is only displayed for half of the flux axis. (e–f) Calculated potentials and wavefunctions based on the fits to the spectra in (c) and (d), respectively.

FIG. 4. (a) Measured qubit lifetime, T1, as a function of Φbias for a fixed Φctrl = 0.378 Φ0. Dashed lines indicate the fitted contributions from individual noise sources (see legend), while the solid lines represent the total predicted T1. (b-e) Lifetimes for four previously characterized bias points of Φctrl, see Fig. 2(a), overlaid with the total predicted noise contribution (solid).

FIG. 5. Fluxonium qubit spectrum and T1 simulation. (a) Fluxonium qubit transition frequency spectrum. (b) Poten- tial and wavefunctions of the fluxonium qubit at the symme- try point (half a flux quantum). (c) Simulated contributions of individual noise sources for the fluxonium qubit (dashed lines), using the same noise models and parameters extracted from fits to the measured cos(2φ) qubit, along with the total noise contribution (solid line).
